planting.note: Plant tubers or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; coral vine is fast-growing and can cover a fence or trellis in a single season. The tuberous roots store carbohydrates and allow rapid regrowth from the base after frost damage in zone 7–8.

directions: Plant tubers in well-drained soil in full sun on a fence, trellis, or arbor; coral vine is one of the most beloved and reliable vining plants of the Gulf South — a fast-growing, drought-tolerant vine from Mexico that covers itself in sprays of small heart-shaped pink to magenta flowers in summer through frost, creating a solid curtain of pink that is irresistible to bees, particularly bumble bees and honeybees. The vine climbs by tendrils and can reach 20–30 feet when established. In zone 7–9 the tops die back in hard frost but established tuberous roots resprout vigorously each spring, often growing 10–15 feet in a single season. The flowers and tubers are edible (tubers roasted like sweet potatoes in Mexico). A white-flowered form ('Album') is less vigorous but charming. A fixture in traditional Gulf South gardens for generations.

planting.note: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5) soil or potting mix in full sun to partial shade; mandevilla requires warm temperatures above 50°F at all times — cold damage occurs below 45°F. In zone 9–10 it may be grown in the ground in a protected location; elsewhere treat as a container plant brought indoors in winter.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; mandevilla is one of the most popular tropical vines for patio containers in the United States, producing large, trumpet-shaped flowers 3–4 inches across in brilliant pink, red, or white from spring through frost. The climbing stems have glossy, dark green leaves that are attractive even out of bloom. Modern compact ('Sundaville' and 'Sun Parasol') series stay under 3 feet and are outstanding in large pots and hanging baskets. The larger vining types need a trellis or support and can reach 8–10 feet. Feed every 2 weeks with a bloom-booster fertilizer for maximum flower production. In zone 9–10 mandevilla may be left outdoors year-round in a sheltered location; in zone 8a bring inside at first frost and keep barely moist through winter. Very sap is milky white and mildly toxic — wear gloves when pruning.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Rangoon creeper is a vigorous vine requiring a sturdy trellis or arbor — established plants can cover a fence or shed in a season. In zone 8–9 tops die back in frost but established roots resprout in spring.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun on a sturdy trellis; Rangoon creeper is one of the most spectacular and fragrant of all tropical vines, producing pendant clusters of fragrant tubular flowers that open pure white, then change through pink to brilliant red over 3–4 days — the different-colored flowers at different stages create a stunning three-color display on the vine at all times during the long blooming season from late spring through fall. The sweet fragrance is strongest in the evening and carries widely on still air. In zone 8–9 established plants die to the ground in freezes but resprout vigorously in spring from the established root system. The vine can become very large in warm climates and may need annual pruning to keep manageable. Once established it is drought tolerant. The seeds are edible and have been used medicinally in Asia for centuries.

common_name: Bengal Clock Vine (Sky Vine, Blue Trumpet Vine)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; bengal clock vine grows extremely rapidly and requires a very sturdy, large trellis, pergola, or fence — it can cover a large structure in a single season and is considered invasive in Florida and Hawaii. Do not plant near natural areas.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun on a very large, sturdy support structure; the Bengal clock vine (sky vine) is one of the most spectacular flowering vines available for warm climates, producing pendant clusters of enormous (3–4 inch), lavender-blue to pale blue funnel-shaped flowers with white throats continuously from spring through frost in zone 9–11. The huge, heart-shaped leaves and rampant growth create a dense, cooling shade canopy over pergolas and arbors very quickly. It is an outstanding choice for covering large unsightly structures rapidly. However, this vine is INVASIVE in Florida, Hawaii, and similar warm climates — plant only where you can control its spread and never near waterways or natural vegetation. In zone 9a it often dies back to established roots in hard winters and recovers rapidly; in true zone 10+ it is a permanent, massive vine.

planting.note: In zone 8–10, plant in fall for a winter-spring bloom season; zonal geraniums perform best in cool weather (50–70°F) and decline or stop flowering in summer heat above 85°F. In zone 9a, fall-through-spring is the preferred season. Start cuttings in late summer to have blooming plants ready for fall planting.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; despite being called 'geraniums,' these plants are Pelargonium — South African natives in the family Geraniaceae. They are among the most popular and widely grown bedding plants in the world, producing heads of bright red, pink, salmon, white, or bicolor flowers on compact mounds of velvety, horseshoe-zoned foliage. In zone 9a, where summers are too hot for them to thrive, they are grown as cool-season annuals from fall through spring — planted in October and blooming through March or April before summer heat causes decline. Take cuttings in late summer before heat ends the season, root them indoors or in shade, and use them as next fall's transplants. Excellent for containers, window boxes, and cool-season borders.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; scented geraniums are more heat tolerant than zonal geraniums and can be maintained through Gulf Coast summers with afternoon shade and adequate water.

directions: Root cuttings in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to light shade; scented geraniums are a diverse group of Pelargonium species grown primarily for their extraordinary aromatic foliage rather than their flowers. The rose-scented P. graveolens is the classic, with deeply lobed leaves smelling of rose-lemon; other varieties offer scents of peppermint, cinnamon, nutmeg, coconut, apple, apricot, pine, and many other aromas, achieved by rubbing or crushing the leaves. The dried leaves are used to flavor sugar, pound cake, jams, and teas; fresh leaves infuse a subtle rose flavor into cream, sorbet, and pound cake. They are more heat-tolerant than zonal geraniums and can survive Gulf Coast summers in partial shade with adequate water. All scented geraniums root easily from cuttings taken in late summer to bring indoors for the winter in zone 8a.

common_name: Beach Morning Glory (Railroad Vine, Bay Hops)

planting.note: Sow scarified seeds directly in sandy, well-drained, infertile soil in full sun; nick the hard seed coat or soak in water 24 hours before planting. Beach morning glory requires pure beach sand or sandy coastal soil — it will not tolerate clay or fertile soil, and actually grows best in the poorest, driest, most exposed coastal conditions.

directions: Sow scarified seeds in sandy, infertile coastal soil in full sun; beach morning glory (railroad vine) is one of the most important native plants of tropical and subtropical beaches and coastal dunes worldwide — a prostrate, creeping vine that can spread 60 feet or more along the sand from a single plant, rooting as it goes, stabilizing shifting dunes against erosion with a mat of large, distinctive bilobed hoofprint-shaped leaves. The large, rose-purple morning glory flowers are produced throughout the growing season and are a beautiful ornamental bonus. Each stem can extend 100 feet, rooting at nodes wherever moisture is available. It is resistant to salt spray, storm surge flooding, and hurricane-force winds in ways that few other plants can match. An outstanding native for coastal restoration, living stabilization of beach dunes, and coastal ornamental planting. It is not invasive in its native coastal range.

common_name: Mexican Flame Vine (Orange Glow Vine, Fireman's Cap)

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Mexican flame vine grows rapidly and blooms from mid-summer through winter in zone 9–11. In zone 8a it may die back in severe frost but resprouts from established roots. Provide a trellis, fence, or large shrub to climb through.

directions: Root cuttings in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun on a fence or trellis; Mexican flame vine is one of the most spectacular winter-blooming vines for zone 9–11, producing dense clusters of bright orange, daisy-like flowers with contrasting yellow or orange centers from October through March — exactly the cool-season period when most other tropical vines have stopped blooming. The loose, twining stems 6–15 feet long bear small, dark green, slightly succulent leaves and are covered in vibrant orange blooms that attract butterflies. The orange color is exceptionally vivid and warm, excellent for combining with blue salvias or purple queen for autumn and winter color. In zone 8a it is killed to the ground in hard freezes but established root systems resprout reliably each spring to bloom again by mid-summer.

common_name: Chalice Vine (Cup of Gold, Hawaiian Wedding Flower)

planting.note: Root woody cuttings in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic, rich soil in full sun; chalice vine is slow to establish but eventually forms a massive, woody-stemmed vine requiring a very large, sturdy support. Bloom is best on established plants and often increases when the plant experiences mild drought stress in late summer-fall.

directions: Plant in fertile, rich, well-drained soil in full sun; chalice vine is one of the most spectacular large-flowering plants in the world, producing enormous chalice-shaped flowers 8–10 inches across in golden yellow with purple veins and a powerful, tropical, coconut-like fragrance, strongest at night. The massive, woody vines with large, glossy evergreen leaves can eventually cover a large wall, pergola, or building. It is one of the showiest of all tropical vines for zone 10–11 landscapes and a traditional feature of California and Florida coastal gardens. Established plants are surprisingly drought tolerant. Bloom is typically heaviest in late winter through spring and again in fall. Caution: all parts of this plant, including the nectar, are highly toxic — the active alkaloids can cause severe poisoning. Do not plant where children, pets, or livestock can access the plant.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; purple wreath prefers bright, hot sun and blooms most prolifically when slightly drought stressed between rain events. In zone 9b–10 it may survive mild winters; established plants freeze-killed in zone 9a often resprout from roots.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; purple wreath is one of the most spectacular tropical flowering vines, producing cascading clusters 12–18 inches long of small, star-shaped violet-purple flowers with contrasting deeper purple calyces that remain decorative for weeks after the petals fall — the persistent purple calyces give the vine a second ornamental period. The rough-textured, sandpaper-like leaves give rise to the alternate name 'sandpaper vine.' Established plants bloom in a spectacular spring flush with intermittent repeat bloom through summer and fall. The woody vine can reach 20–40 feet and requires a large, sturdy support. It is drought tolerant once established and blooms most prolifically with minimal irrigation. A stunning specimen for zone 10–12 landscapes; in zone 9b it can survive in sheltered south-facing locations.
